$comment: Example layout demonstrating BOTH per-record prefixes: a 5-byte opaque leading-byte strip + a 4-byte mainframe-style RDW (rdw1=2 bytes, rdw2=2 bytes, binary little-endian uint). Order on the wire is: [strip_leading_bytes (5)][rdw (4)][TU4R...][segments...][ENDS][delimiter]. The engine consumes 9 leading bytes per record before it sees the first segment header. Segment definitions mirror the realistic fixture (examples/sample_a.dat layouts) so you can drop this in as a layout_file_A.json variant when the source feed wraps each record in these prefixes. Set 'strip_leading_bytes' or 'rdw' to null individually to opt out of either prefix; the on-wire bytes the engine actually parses (TU4R..ENDS) are unchanged.

strip_leading_bytes.$comment: 5 opaque bytes consumed at the head of each record, before RDW. Common use: a block-control word from upstream that the engine doesn't need to interpret. 'encoding' is informational only; the engine reads size raw bytes and discards them.

rdw.$comment: Classic mainframe RDW: 2-byte length + 2-byte reserved, unsigned little-endian uint. Total 4 bytes consumed per record after strip_leading_bytes and before TU4R. Engine does not validate rdw1 against the actual record length — the bytes are simply skipped.
